mnemonics.title: P = Polynomial time – Quick & Easy
mnemonics.content: P stands for Polynomial time: problems solvable in O(n^k) for some constant k. Mnemonic: think of the letter P as for 'Pizza' – you can bake it in polynomial time.

mnemonics.title: NP = Non‑deterministic Polynomial – Lucky Guess
mnemonics.content: NP stands for Non‑deterministic Polynomial time: problems where a guessed solution can be verified quickly. Mnemonic: 'N' for 'Not sure', 'P' for 'Proof' – you guess the proof then check.

mnemonics.title: NP‑Complete: Never Prioritize, Check Thoroughly
mnemonics.content: NP‑Complete (NPC) problems are the hardest in NP; any NP problem can be reduced to them in polynomial time. Mnemonic: NPC = 'No Problem Can be left unsolved' – solve one, solve all NP.

mnemonics.title: NP‑Hard: Not a Piece of Cake
mnemonics.content: NP‑Hard problems are at least as hard as the hardest problems in NP. Mnemonic: NP‑Hard = 'No Probable Hope' – they are beyond NP.

mnemonics.title: Cook‑Levin Theorem – Cooking Up SAT
mnemonics.content: The Cook‑Levin theorem shows SAT is NP‑Complete. Mnemonic: 'Cook' = SAT – the sauce that makes a problem NP‑Complete.

mnemonics.title: P vs NP Question – Pizza or No Pizza?
mnemonics.content: The P versus NP problem asks whether every quickly verifiable problem is also quickly solvable. Mnemonic: think of 'P' as Pizza (easy) and 'NP' as No Pizza (hard) – we don't know if we can get pizza quickly.

acronyms.usage: Class of problems solvable in O(n^k) time for some constant k.
Class of problems for which a given solution can be verified in polynomial time.
Subset of NP containing the hardest problems; any NP problem can be reduced to an NPC problem in polynomial time.
Problems at least as hard as the hardest problems in NP; not necessarily in NP.
The first problem proven NP‑Complete; used as a canonical NP‑Complete problem for reductions.

visualTips.visualization: Draw three concentric circles: the outer circle is NP, the inner circle is P, and the boundary region where NP touches P is NP‑Complete. NP‑Hard surrounds NP‑Complete outside the NP circle.
Imagine a 'translator' machine that takes any NP problem as input and outputs an equivalent instance of SAT (or another NP‑Complete problem) in polynomial time.
Decision problems are yes/no toggle switches; optimization problems are volume knobs seeking the best value.
Picture a layered cake: base layer = P, middle layer = NP, top frosting = NP‑Complete, and the cherry on top beyond the frosting = NP‑Hard.

analogies.analogy: Verifying a puzzle solution is like checking that all pieces fit – you can quickly confirm correctness, but finding the solution from scratch may take forever.
They act like a master key: solving one NP‑Complete problem opens the door to solving any problem in NP.
Climbing Everest without oxygen – far harder than any NP problem; exact solutions in polynomial time are unrealistic.
It is like asking whether finding a password by trial (hard) is as easy as verifying a given password (easy) – we currently do not know.
